request.question: Who published the comic book in which Jean Loring was a supporting character and primary love interest of The Atom (Dr. Ray Palmer) is a fictional superhero appearing in American comic books?

title: Rumiko Fujikawa
Jean Loring
Barbara Gordon
Lois Lane
Atom (Ray Palmer)
Snapper Carr
Spider-Man
Superman
Nova (Richard Rider)
Valkyrie (Marvel Comics)
sentences: Rumiko Fujikawa is a fictional character, a supporting character appearing in American comic books published by Marvel Comics.
Created by writer Kurt Busiek and artist Sean Chen, the character made her first appearance in "The Invincible Iron Man" vol.
A supporting character of Iron Man, she was Tony's primary love interest throughout the duration of the series.
Jean Loring is a fictional character in comic books published by DC Comics, formerly associated with superhero the Atom for whom she was a supporting character and primary love interest.
She first appeared in "Showcase" #34 (September–October 1961), created by Gardner Fox and Gil Kane.
The character appeared continually in minor roles until the 2004 storyline "Identity Crisis", in which she suffered a mental breakdown and murdered Sue Dibny, wife of the Elongated Man.
This would later lead Loring to assume the mantle of the supervillain Eclipso.
Barbara Gordon is a fictional superhero appearing in American comic books published by DC Comics, commonly in association with the superhero Batman.
The character was created by William Dozier, Julius Schwartz, and Carmine Infantino.

At the request of the producers of the 1960s "Batman" television series, DC editor Schwartz called for a new female counterpart to the superhero Batman that could be introduced into publication and the third season of the show simultaneously.
The character subsequently made her first comic book appearance as Batgirl in "Detective Comics" #359, titled "The Million Dollar Debut of Batgirl!"
(January 1967), by writer Gardner Fox and artist Carmine Infantino.
Lois Lane is a fictional character appearing in American comic books published by DC Comics.
Created by writer Jerry Siegel and artist Joe Shuster, she first appeared in "Action Comics" #1 (June 1938).
Lois is a reporter for the Metropolis newspaper, the "Daily Planet", an award-winning journalist and the primary love interest of the superhero Superman.
The Atom (Dr. Ray Palmer) is a fictional superhero appearing in American comic books published by DC Comics.
The character was created by editor and co-plotter Julius Schwartz, writer Gardner Fox and penciler Gil Kane.
The Atom was one of the first superheroes of the Silver Age of comic books and debuted in "Showcase" #34 (October 1961).
Lucas "Snapper" Carr is a fictional character appearing in American comic books published by DC Comics.

The character, whose fictional nickname is almost always used by other characters in favor of his given name, was created by Gardner Fox (writer) and Mike Sekowsky (penciller), and made his first appearance in "The Brave and the Bold" in February 1960.
From 1960 to 1969, Snapper Carr appeared as a supporting character to the Justice League of America, a superhero team.
The character occasionally appeared in comics featuring the Justice League from 1969 to 1989, when the "Invasion!
" limited-series comic book gave him superpowers.
He was associated with a new superhero team, The Blasters, in various comics until 1993, when he lost his powers and became a main character in the "Hourman" comic book.
After the cancellation of "Hourman" in April 2001, he became a main character in the "Young Justice" comic book beginning in December 2001.

"Young Justice" was cancelled in May 2003, and he became associated with the governmental organization Checkmate, a role revealed when the character played a small but important role in the 2007-2008 limited series comic book "52 Aftermath: The Four Horsemen".
The character made major appearances in "Final Crisis: Resist" in December 2008 and "Justice League of America 80-Page Giant" in November 2009.
Spider-Man is a fictional superhero appearing in American comic books published by Marvel Comics.
The character was created by writer-editor Stan Lee and writer-artist Steve Ditko, and first appeared in the anthology comic book "Amazing Fantasy" #15 (August 1962) in the Silver Age of Comic Books.

Lee and Ditko conceived the character as an orphan being raised by his Aunt May and Uncle Ben in New York City after his parents Richard and Mary Parker were killed in a plane crash, and as a teenager, having to deal with the normal struggles of adolescence in addition to those of a costumed crime-fighter.

Spider-Man's creators gave him super strength and agility, the ability to cling to most surfaces, shoot spider-webs using wrist-mounted devices of his own invention, which he calls "web-shooters", and react to danger quickly with his "spider-sense", enabling him to combat his foes.
Superman is a fictional superhero appearing in American comic books published by DC Comics.
The character was created by writer Jerry Siegel and artist Joe Shuster, high school students living in Cleveland, Ohio, in 1933.
They sold Superman to Detective Comics, the future DC Comics, in 1938.
Superman debuted in "Action Comics" #1 (cover-dated June 1938) and subsequently appeared in various radio serials, newspaper strips, television programs, films, and video games.
With this success, Superman helped to create the superhero archetype and establish its primacy within the American comic book.
The character is also referred to by such epithets as the Big Blue Boy Scout, the Man of Steel, the Man of Tomorrow, and the Last Son of Krypton.
Nova (Richard Rider) is a fictional superhero appearing in American comic books published by Marvel Comics.
The character appeared historically as the star of his own series, and at other times, as a supporting character in team books such as "The New Warriors".
He is a member of the intergalactic police force known as the Nova Corps, for which he gained superhuman abilities including enhanced strength, flight and resistance to injury.
Valkyrie is a fictional superhero appearing in American comic books published by Marvel Comics.
The character was created by Roy Thomas and John Buscema, based on the Norse mythological figure Brynhildr.
The character first appeared in "The Avengers" #83 (December 1970) and has become a mainstay of the superhero team known as the Defenders.
An Asgardian by birth, Valkyrie, also known by her real name, Brunnhilde, was selected by Odin to lead the Valkyrior.
Among her other aliases are Barbara Denton-Norriss, Samantha Parrington, Sian Bowen, and Annabelle Riggs who were all host bodies to the spirit of Brunnhilde.
Samantha Parrington, a previous host of Brunnhilde, later received Valkyrie's powers and became a member of the Defenders herself.
An ally and one-time love interest of Thor, she was ranked 30th in IGN's list of "The Top 50 Avengers", and 65th in "Comics Buyer's Guide's" "100 Sexiest Women in Comics" list.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Task: Compress the grounded first-hop evidence into a single concise, retrieval-oriented intermediate state to guide the next-hop retrieval. Do not produce the final answer or perform any new retrieval here.

Required content of `summary` (single field):
- A short header identifying the primary topic(s) found in the passages (1 line).
- A concise enumeration of the concrete facts supported verbatim by the provided passages: list entity mentions and directly supported factual spans (dates, years, titles, roles). Use very short quoted fragments (<= ~15 words) only when those words appear verbatim in the passages. Keep to facts present in the passages; do not add or infer new facts.
- Explicitly expose any ambiguity or missing information that blocks answering: if multiple candidate entities or page variants appear, enumerate those candidates and label the name as ambiguous; if a required target fact is missing or ambiguous, label it as the missing fact and identify the bridge entity that connects this hop to the next.
- Prioritized next-hop retrieval directives (1–4 items): concrete page titles or disambiguation pages to fetch, explicit article sections to inspect (e.g., lede/infobox, "Career", "Publication", "History"), or tight search keywords. Order them by priority and be specific (include possible disambiguation terms or section names).

Formatting and style constraints:
- Keep the entire `summary` concise: a short header plus 1–4 brief sentences or 2–6 short bullet lines in total.
- Preserve the habit of extracting and citing concrete facts verbatim from the passages (names, dates, passage labels).
- Do not output a final answer, comparison, or any numeric fact that purports to resolve the question here. Even if the passages fully and unambiguously contain all required facts, present that evidence and explicitly state "no additional retrieval needed," but defer producing any final answer to a downstream component.
- If a name or term has multiple distinct variants in the passages, do not pick one without disambiguating evidence; enumerate candidates instead.
- Do not invent or add facts not present in the supplied passages. Avoid long verbatim quotations; prefer short extracts (<= ~15 words) or paraphrase.

Output expectations: produce exactly one field `summary` containing the concise, retrieval-oriented summary that meets the above requirements.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact, retrieval-focused second-hop query in the field `query`. Use only the provided `question` and `summary_1` as inputs. Do not include any explanation, answer, fallback text, lists, or additional fields — the output must be a single one-line query string.

When composing the query, select the minimal anchor token(s) visible in `question` and/or `summary_1` that point to the missing page or fact. You may append a single short attribute keyword (for example country member occupations record season) only when that keyword is needed to disambiguate the evidence type. Do not introduce any other new tokens.

Decision templates (choose the single template that matches the missing evidence implied by summary_1):
- Connector between two named items: emit a short interrogative naming both items and requesting the linking evidence (e.g., artist album recording) in one line. Retain the visible item tokens from the inputs.
- Metadata for a single anchor title: emit a compact keyword-style query consisting of the anchor plus brief attribute keywords (for example: artist genre).
- Temporal or aggregate fact: include the year or season token plus the entity type and a short cue such as season count.

Use these operational rules when choosing a template:
- If summary_1 explicitly lists two or more distinct page anchors as next-hop targets, combine those visible anchor tokens into one compact multi-anchor query and append a short attribute keyword naming the evidence type (for example country member occupations record).
- If summary_1 flags a single missing anchor or a missing_titles list is present, emit a single-anchor metadata-style query that places the missing anchor token first and appends a concise attribute keyword naming the desired fact.
- When summary_1 names multiple candidate entities and the missing fact concerns one candidate’s membership, order, or relation to a canonical collection, prefer a connector-style query that pairs the highest-priority candidate token (first or prioritized in summary_1) with the canonical collection token plus a short attribute keyword requesting membership or order.
- When individual member tokens are present but the needed retrieval is about that individual, prefer member-token + group-anchor + member (or equivalent short attribute) to target the individual’s page rather than issuing a broad group-level query.

Strict formatting rules:
- Emit exactly one line and exactly one string in `query` containing only the query text.
- Keep the query compact (aim for ≤ 10–12 words/tokens) and free of punctuation or explanatory clauses.
- Preserve visible tokens from `question` and `summary_1` (title words, years, or entity names) to anchor retrieval.
- Do not answer, conclude, enumerate candidates, assert hidden facts, or include user-facing instructions.

Produce the `query` field now.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Instructions (must follow exactly):
- Emit a single field named `summary` containing only 1–3 concise sentences that integrate the first-hop `context` and the second-hop `passages` into a grounded post-retrieval state.
- If the `context` already contains a concise answer sentence, keep that sentence as the first sentence of `summary` unless the `passages` clearly contradict it; append supporting sentence(s) after it rather than replacing it.
- Preserve the primary answer name(s) exactly as they appear in the inputs (context or passages) unless those names clearly conflict with stronger passage content.
- When one or more `passages` supply short biographical, membership, partnership, or corroborating facts about a named entity from the `context`, append at most one brief supporting sentence per relevant named person. Each such supporting sentence should:
  - begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the passages (if available),
  - paraphrase up to one or two key attributes present in the passages (examples: nationality, award/winner status, current affiliation/team, partner identity, founding/formation fact, birth date), and
  - avoid long verbatim copying (prefer paraphrase; use short quotes only when a brief exact phrase from a passage is necessary for clarity).
- If the question requires naming both members of a partnership or collaborator pair, name both people in the summary and, if passages provide partner-specific attributes, include one brief attribute for each as separate short clauses or sentences if needed.
- Do not invent facts not present in the provided passages. If passages conflict on a specific attribute, do not synthesize a new reconciled fact; instead prefer the passage that most directly addresses the question or omit the disputed attribute (or state necessary ambiguity only when needed to avoid a false claim).
- Keep the `summary` compact and focused: avoid extraneous biography, discography/chart data, or unrelated commentary. When `context` is absent or is not a concise answer, produce a 1–2 sentence integrated summary naming the relevant entity(ies) and including at most one brief paraphrased attribute drawn from the passages.

Additional constraints (must follow):
- The first sentence must be an answer-first concise identification (a direct answer or concise identification). If the `context` already contains such a sentence, preserve it exactly unless a passage explicitly and directly contradicts it; otherwise derive that first sentence from the clearest passage.
- After the first sentence, append at most one short supporting sentence for each additional relevant named entity only when the passages supply corroborating attributes. Each supporting sentence must begin with the entity's canonical name as shown in the inputs (context or passages) and paraphrase no more than one or two simple attributes.
- When a queried binary/status attribute for an entity is not stated in the provided passages, indicate that absence concisely within that entity's supporting sentence (for example, with a short parenthetical or clause saying the status is not given in the provided evidence).
- If the passages explicitly assert a relationship between two or more entities that is directly relevant to the question, include one short relationship sentence after the per-entity supporting sentence(s) describing that relationship; do not embed the same relationship claim redundantly in multiple sentences.
- If relevant entities exceed the available supporting-sentence budget under the 1–3 sentence limit, prioritize entities in this order: entities explicitly named in the question, entities named in the preserved first sentence, then other context entities. Do not exceed the 1–3 sentence cap.

Output requirement: exactly one JSON field `summary` whose value is the integrated 1–3 sentence summary.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the fields `answer`.

Instructions:
- Use only `summary_1` and `summary_2` as the evidential basis for the final decision; do not add new facts or external information.
- First determine whether the question is a binary (yes/no) proposition and whether both `summary_1` and `summary_2` clearly and unambiguously support the same binary outcome.
- If BOTH summaries unambiguously assert the same binary conclusion, emit exactly one lowercase token as the entire content of `answer`: use "yes" for an affirmative conclusion and "no" for a negative conclusion. The `answer` field must contain that single token and nothing else (no punctuation, no extra whitespace, no explanation).
- Prefer `summary_2` as the decisive statement when it clarifies or restates `summary_1`, but still require that both summaries support the same binary judgment before emitting the single-token `answer`.
- If the summaries conflict, if either summary hedges, is ambiguous, silent about the binary relation, or otherwise fails to clearly agree, do NOT apply the single-token rule; instead produce the module's normal fallback/unresolved output for `answer`.
- Do not infer that the final state is unresolved merely because `summary_1` uses earlier unresolved or bridge language; prefer `summary_2` when it clarifies `summary_1`.
- Always remain truthful to the visible summaries and do not alter their factual conclusions.

Binary detection is mandatory and must run first. Only if the question is not a binary yes/no or the binary detector does not apply, proceed to the extractive rules below. Use the extractor that matches the question intent and only apply an extractor when the summaries present the required adjacent or verbatim phrasing.

Extraction and formatting rules (apply only when binary detection does not produce a single-token answer):
- Person-name selection: if a longer verbatim personal-name string appears in either summary and contains a shorter candidate as a substring, emit the longest verbatim name exactly as it appears in the summaries. Prefer wording from `summary_2` when both summaries provide the same evidence but differ in form.
- Denomination/type extraction: for questions asking a "kind", "type", or "denomination" of a role, extract and emit exactly the minimal token or short phrase that appears immediately adjacent to the role word in the summaries (the word/phrase answering "what kind").
- Event-name extraction: when summaries show a contiguous proper-name followed by a generic event-type noun, emit only the contiguous proper-name core. Strip any leading articles and remove the trailing generic event-type noun; emit only the proper-name portion exactly as given in the summaries.
- Nationality handling: if both summaries unambiguously state the same nationality phrase and at least one summary places the literal single word "citizen" immediately after that nationality phrase, append exactly one space and the single word "citizen" to the nationality phrase. Otherwise emit the nationality phrase exactly as given in the summaries.

General formatting constraints for all outputs:
- Always emit the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries and do not add explanation, context, punctuation, or extra words.
- Do not introduce external facts, additional descriptors, dates, roles, or explanatory text not present in the summaries.
- Do not normalize or invent name variants that are not verbatim in the summaries.
- If the summaries conflict, lack the required adjacent phrasing, or do not present a clear verbatim form for the requested attribute, do not guess—use the module's normal unresolved output.

Output contract:
- Produce a single field `answer` whose contents follow the rules above. The `answer` field must contain only the minimal string supported by `summary_1` and `summary_2` and no additional tokens, punctuation, or explanation.

response.hop1_query: Who published the comic book in which Jean Loring was a supporting character and primary love interest of The Atom (Dr. Ray Palmer) is a fictional superhero appearing in American comic books?
hop1_docs: Jean Loring | Jean Loring is a fictional character in comic books published by DC Comics, formerly associated with superhero the Atom for whom she was a supporting character and primary love interest.  She first appeared in "Showcase" #34 (September–October 1961), created by Gardner Fox and Gil Kane.  The character appeared continually in minor roles until the 2004 storyline "Identity Crisis", in which she suffered a mental breakdown and murdered Sue Dibny, wife of the Elongated Man.  This would later lead Loring to assume the mantle of the supervillain Eclipso.

Atom (Ray Palmer) | The Atom (Dr. Ray Palmer) is a fictional superhero appearing in American comic books published by DC Comics.  The character was created by editor and co-plotter Julius Schwartz, writer Gardner Fox and penciler Gil Kane.  The Atom was one of the first superheroes of the Silver Age of comic books and debuted in "Showcase" #34 (October 1961).

Lois Lane | Lois Lane is a fictional character appearing in American comic books published by DC Comics.  Created by writer Jerry Siegel and artist Joe Shuster, she first appeared in "Action Comics" #1 (June 1938).  Lois is a reporter for the Metropolis newspaper, the "Daily Planet", an award-winning journalist and the primary love interest of the superhero Superman.

Rumiko Fujikawa | Rumiko Fujikawa is a fictional character, a supporting character appearing in American comic books published by Marvel Comics.  Created by writer Kurt Busiek and artist Sean Chen, the character made her first appearance in "The Invincible Iron Man" vol.  3 #4 (May 1998).  A supporting character of Iron Man, she was Tony's primary love interest throughout the duration of the series.

Elektra (comics) | Elektra Natchios ( , ) is a fictional character appearing in American comic books published by Marvel Comics.  Created by Frank Miller, the character first appeared in "Daredevil" #168 (January 1981).  She is a love interest of the superhero Daredevil, but her violent nature and mercenary lifestyle divide the two.

Lucius Fox | Lucius Fox is a fictional character appearing American comic books published by DC Comics, commonly in association with the superhero Batman.  As a supporting character in the comics, he acts as Bruce Wayne's business manager at Wayne Enterprises who runs the business interests that supply Batman's equipment needs as well as financing his operations.

Claire Temple | Claire Temple is a fictional character appearing in American comic books published by Marvel Comics.  An African-American doctor, she is primarily affiliated with the superhero Luke Cage and is one of his early love interests.
response.summary_1:
Primary topics: Jean Loring; The Atom (Dr. Ray Palmer) — publisher information.

- Jean Loring — "fictional character in comic books published by DC Comics"; "supporting character and primary love interest" of the Atom; "first appeared in 'Showcase' #34 (September–October 1961)"; "created by Gardner Fox and Gil Kane."
- The Atom (Ray Palmer) — "fictional superhero appearing in American comic books published by DC Comics"; "debuted in 'Showcase' #34 (October 1961)"; created by "Julius Schwartz, Gardner Fox and Gil Kane."
- Ambiguity/missing facts: none for the publisher — both passages explicitly state "published by DC Comics." (Minor date-wording variant: Jean Loring cited as "September–October 1961" vs Atom "October 1961" — not relevant to the publisher fact.)
- Next-hop retrieval directives (priority order):
  1. No additional retrieval needed — use the provided passages' ledes for publisher confirmation.
  2. (Optional verification) Fetch "Jean Loring" page — inspect lede/infobox and "Publication history" or "Biography" sections.
  3. (Optional verification) Fetch "Atom (Ray Palmer)" page — inspect lede/infobox and "Publication history" or "Character overview" sections.
hop2_docs: Jean Loring | Jean Loring is a fictional character in comic books published by DC Comics, formerly associated with superhero the Atom for whom she was a supporting character and primary love interest.  She first appeared in "Showcase" #34 (September–October 1961), created by Gardner Fox and Gil Kane.  The character appeared continually in minor roles until the 2004 storyline "Identity Crisis", in which she suffered a mental breakdown and murdered Sue Dibny, wife of the Elongated Man.  This would later lead Loring to assume the mantle of the supervillain Eclipso.
Gloria Loring | Gloria Loring-Lagler (born Gloria Jean Goff; December 10, 1946) is an American singer and actress.  She is known for playing Liz Chandler on "Days of Our Lives" for five years.

Lands of Lore (series) | Lands of Lore or LoL is a role-playing video game series by Westwood Studios.  The first two games in the series were published by Virgin Interactive, while the third was published by Electronic Arts following their acquisition of Westwood Studios.  "Lands of Lore" follows the tradition of "Dungeon Master", but introduced a linear scenario-based storyline, rather than characters and feats.
Forbidden Lore | Forbidden Lore is an accessory for the 2nd edition of the "Advanced Dungeons & Dragons" fantasy role-playing game, published in 1992.

London Lore | London lore: the legends and traditions of the world's most vibrant city is a 2008 book by Steve Roud, concerning the folklore and history of London.  Another edition was published in 2010.

Lands of Lore: The Throne of Chaos | Lands of Lore: The Throne of Chaos is a 1993 role-playing video game developed by Westwood Studios and published by Virgin Games for MS-DOS, the NEC PC-9801, and FM Towns.  It was the first installment of the "Lands of Lore" series.  The player travels around various environments, collecting items and battling monsters in an attempt to save the kingdom from a witch named Scotia, who has acquired shapeshifting abilities.

Times of Lore | Times of Lore is a 1988 action role-playing game that was developed and published by Origin Systems for several platforms, including PC, Commodore 64/128, ZX Spectrum, Amstrad, Atari ST, Apple II, NES, and Amiga.

response.summary_2: Jean Loring is a fictional character in comic books published by DC Comics. Jean Loring first appeared in Showcase #34 (September–October 1961) and was created by Gardner Fox and Gil Kane.
